title: Piet
content:
آ آ آ آ I do not love my Empireï؟½s foes,
آ آ آ آ Nor call ï؟½em angels; still,
آ آ آ آ What is the sense of ï؟½atinï؟½ those
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oom you are paid to kill?
آ آ آ آ So, barrinï؟½ all that foreign lot
آ آ آ آ Which only joined for spite,
آ آ آ آ Myself, Iï؟½d just as soon as not
آ آ آ آ Respect the man I fight.
آ آ آ آ Ah there, Piet!, ï؟½is trousies to ï؟½is knees,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Is coat-tails lyinï؟½ level in the bullet-sprinkled breeze;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½E does not lose ï؟½is rifle anï؟½ ï؟½e does not lose ï؟½is seat,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve known a lot oï؟½ people ride a damï؟½ sight worse than Piet.

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve ï؟½eard ï؟½im cryinï؟½ from the ground
آ آ آ آ Like Abelï؟½s blood of old,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ skirmished out to look, anï؟½ found
آ آ آ آ The beggar nearly cold.
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve waited on till ï؟½e was dead
آ آ آ آ (Which couldnï؟½t ï؟½elp ï؟½im much),
آ آ آ آ But many grateful things ï؟½e ï؟½s said
آ آ آ آ To me for doinï؟½ such.
آ آ آ آ Ah there, Piet! whose time ï؟½as come to die,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Is carcase past rebellion, but ï؟½is eyes inquirinï؟½ why.
آ آ آ آ Though dressed in stolen uniform with badge oï؟½ rank complete,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve known a lot oï؟½ fellers go a damï؟½ sight worse than Piet.

آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ when there was nï؟½t aught to do
آ آ آ آ But camp and cattle-guards,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve fought with ï؟½im the ï؟½ole day through
آ آ آ آ At fifteen ï؟½undred yards;
آ آ آ آ Long afternoons oï؟½ lyinï؟½ still,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ ï؟½earinï؟½ as you lay
آ آ آ آ The bullets swish from ï؟½ill to ï؟½ill
آ آ آ آ Like scythes among the ï؟½ay.
آ آ آ آ Ah there, Piet!-beï؟½ind ï؟½is stony kop.
آ آ آ آ With ï؟½is Boer bread anï؟½ biltong, anï؟½ ï؟½is flask of awful Dop;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Is Mauser for amusement anï؟½ ï؟½is pony for retreat,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve known a lot oï؟½ fellers shoot a damï؟½ sight worse than Piet.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s shoved ï؟½is rifle ï؟½neath my nose
آ آ آ آ Before Iï؟½d time to think,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ borrowed all my Sunday cloï؟½es
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ sent me ï؟½ome in pink;
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ I ï؟½ave crept (Lord, ï؟½ow Iï؟½ve crept!)
آ آ آ آ On ï؟½ands anï؟½ knees Iï؟½ve gone,
آ آ آ آ And spoored and floored and caught and kept
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ sent him to Ceylon!
آ آ آ آ Ah there, Piet!, youï؟½ve sold me many a pup,
آ آ آ آ When week on week alternate it was you anï؟½ me ï؟½ï؟½ands up! ï؟½
آ آ آ آ But though I never made you walk man-naked in the ï؟½eat,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve known a lot of fellows stalk a damï؟½ sight worse than Piet.

آ آ آ آ From Plewmanï؟½s to Marabastad,
آ آ آ آ From Ookiep to De Aar,
آ آ آ آ Me anï؟½ my trusty friend ï؟½ave ï؟½ad,
آ آ آ آ As you might say, a war;
آ آ آ آ But seeinï؟½ what both parties done
آ آ آ آ Before ï؟½e owned defeat,
آ آ آ آ I ainï؟½t more proud of ï؟½avinï؟½ won,
آ آ آ آ Than I am pleased with Piet.
آ آ آ آ Ah there, Piet!, picked up beï؟½ind the drive!
آ آ آ آ The wonder wasnï؟½t ï؟½ow ï؟½e fought, but ï؟½ow ï؟½e kepï؟½ alive,
آ آ آ آ With nothinï؟½ in ï؟½is belly, on ï؟½is back, or to ï؟½is feet,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve known a lot oï؟½ men behave a damï؟½ sight worse than Piet.

آ آ آ آ No more Iï؟½ll ï؟½ear ï؟½is rifle crack
آ آ آ آ Along the blockï؟½ouse fence,
آ آ آ آ The beggarï؟½s on the peaceful tack,
آ آ آ آ Regardless of expense;
آ آ آ آ For countinï؟½ what ï؟½e eats anï؟½ draws,
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ gifts anï؟½ loans as well,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Eï؟½s gettinï؟½ ï؟½alf the Earth, because
آ آ آ آ ï؟½E didnï؟½t give us ï؟½Ell!
آ آ آ آ Ah there, Piet! with your brand-new English plough,
آ آ آ آ Your gratis tents anï؟½ cattle, anï؟½ your most ungrateful frow,
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ve made the British taxpayer rebuild your country seat,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve known some pet battalions charge a damï؟½ sight less than Piet.
meaning: About: Joseph Rudyard Kipling (December 30, 1865 ï؟½ January 18, 1936) was an English author and poet, born in Bombay, India, and best known for his works The Jungle Book (1894), The Second Jungle Book (1895), Just So Stories (1902), and Puck of Pook's Hill (1906); his novel, Kim (1901); his poems, including Mandalay (1890), Gunga Din (1890), and "Ifï؟½" (1910); and his many short stories, including "The Man Who Would Be King" (1888) and the collections Life's Handicap (1891), The Day's Work (1898), and Plain Tales from the Hills (1888). He is regarded as a major "innovator in the art of the short story"; his children's books are enduring classics of children's literature; and his best work speaks to a versatile and luminous narrative gift.
